Routine.Name: Logic
Routine.Description: Divide 64-bit LINT by a 32-bit DINT to give 64-bit (LINT) quotient and 32-bit (DINT) remainder

Rung.Comment:
============================================================ L_DIV : Divide (64-bit by 32-bit) ============================================================ Revision 1.0-00 Release: 2009-Jul-31 Initial Release ============================================================ Divides a LINT (signed 64-bit) dividend by a DINT (signed 32-bit) divisor and returns a LINT (signed 64-bit) quotient and a DINT (signed 32-bit) remainder.
This instruction breaks the LINT divident into three DINTs (24 bits, 20 bits and 20 bits) to do the math in order to avoid generating a Controller MINOR FAULT on overflow. (Sample code provided in RSLogix 5000 Online Help is much smaller and faster, but causes numerous Minor Faults on Overflows -- S:V -- on the low-order difference! The controller must take time to log the minor faults, and many users do not want to see Minor Faults on normal math operations. See the RSLogix 5000 Help Topic "Arithmetic Status Flags" for more information.) =================================================== CONVERT LINT DIVIDENT TO DINTs =================================================== This COPy takes the input dividend LINT and breaks it into a pair of DINTs so we can do math on it. Logix V17 has a LINT data type, but math instructions (and AOI Input and Output Parameters) don't support LINT.

=================================================== CHECK FOR DIVIDE BY ZERO =================================================== If the divisor is zero, simply output the most positive number (if the dividend is positive) or most negative number (if the dividend is negative).
Rung.Text: EQU(Inp_Divisor,0)[XIO(Wrk_InpA[1].31) MOV(16#7fff_ffff,Wrk_OutC[1]) MOV(16#ffff_ffff,Wrk_OutC[0]) MOV(16#7fff_ffff,Wrk_OutRem) ,XIC(Wrk_InpA[1].31) MOV(16#8000_0000,Wrk_OutC[1]) CLR(Wrk_OutC[0]) MOV(16#8000_0000,Wrk_OutRem) ]JMP(End);

Rung.Comment: =================================================== TRACK SIGNS =================================================== If the dividend is negative, track that, and negate it. If the divisor is negative, track that, and negate it. We'll do the division with the unsigned values, then figure out the final signs.
Rung.Text: [XIC(Wrk_InpA[1].31) [NOT(Wrk_InpA[0],Wrk_InpA[0]) NOT(Wrk_InpA[1],Wrk_InpA[1]) ,EQU(Wrk_InpA[0],16#7FFF_FFFF) MOV(16#8000_0000,Wrk_InpA[0]) OTE(Wrk_InpALoOvflw) ,XIO(Wrk_InpALoOvflw) ADD(Wrk_InpA[0],1,Wrk_InpA[0]) ,EQU(Wrk_InpA[0],0) [EQU(Wrk_InpA[1],16#7FFF_FFFF) MOV(16#8000_0000,Wrk_InpA[1]) OTE(Wrk_InpAHiOvflw) ,XIO(Wrk_InpAHiOvflw) ADD(Wrk_InpA[1],1,Wrk_InpA[1]) ] ,OTE(Wrk_A_Sign) ] ,ABS(Inp_Divisor,Wrk_InpB) XIC(Inp_Divisor.31) OTE(Wrk_B_Sign) ];

Rung.Comment:
=================================================== SPREAD 64 BITS ACROSS THREE DINTs =================================================== If we do the math on 32-bit DINTs, the math on the low-order DINT (bits .0 to .31 of the 64-bit value) will cause Overflows, setting the S:V status bit and THROWING A MINOR FAULT.
To avoid the Minor Fault, we take the two DINTs and break them up into THREE: a 24 bit low-order DINT, a 20-bit middle DINT, and a 20-bit high-order DINT.
Doing the math on less than 30 bits per ADD ensures we never have an Overflow that throws a Minor Fault. The BTDs split the 64 bits of Input A among the three DINTs used in the math.
We need to do the same with our 32-bit divisor, because we will be shifting it left in a 64-bit space to align with the dividend for subtraction.

=================================================== LOOP TO SHIFT DIVISOR LEFT =================================================== This instruction uses the grade-school long division method, shifting and subtracting. In preparation for the big shift-subtract loop, shift the divisor left until it is larger than the dividend.
If the shifted divisor is still less than the dividend, we need to shift another position to the left.
When the divisor has been shifted left to be greater than the dividend, we can start to shift right and subtract, just like grade school division.
This rung does the actual bit shift to the left, in the 20 / 20 / 24 bit groups. The "Wrk_index" keeps track of HOW MANY positions we shifted left ("decimal place")
Rung.Text: [BTD(Wrk_B[2],0,Wrk_B[2],1,19) XIC(Wrk_B[1].19) OTE(Wrk_B[2].0) ,BTD(Wrk_B[1],0,Wrk_B[1],1,19) XIC(Wrk_B[0].23) OTE(Wrk_B[1].0) ,BTD(Wrk_B[0],0,Wrk_B[0],1,23) OTU(Wrk_B[0].0) ,ADD(Wrk_index,1,Wrk_index) ,XIO(Wrk_B[2].19) JMP(ShiftLeft) ];

Rung.Comment:
Now start to shift the divisor right and subtract from the dividend.
Do the check for Dividend greater than or equal to Divisor. If it is, we add one to the quotient. (That 1 will get shifted left as we work the index back to 0 and do the subtractions for each "decimal place".
Subtract the shifted divisor from the remaining dividend to determine the remainder for this decimal place.
Rung.Text: XIC(Wrk_A_GEQ_B)[SUB(Wrk_A[0],Wrk_B[0],Wrk_A[0]) ,XIC(Wrk_A[0].24) SUB(Wrk_A[1],1,Wrk_A[1]) ,SUB(Wrk_A[1],Wrk_B[1],Wrk_A[1]) ,XIC(Wrk_A[1].20) SUB(Wrk_A[2],1,Wrk_A[2]) ,SUB(Wrk_A[2],Wrk_B[2],Wrk_A[2]) ,MVM(0,16#ff00_0000,Wrk_A[0]) MVM(0,16#fff0_0000,Wrk_A[1]) ,ADD(Wrk_C[0],1,Wrk_C[0]) ,XIC(Wrk_C[0].24) ADD(Wrk_C[1],1,Wrk_C[1]) ,XIC(Wrk_C[1].20) ADD(Wrk_C[2],1,Wrk_C[2]) ,MVM(0,16#ff00_0000,Wrk_C[0]) MVM(0,16#fff0_0000,Wrk_C[1]) ];

Rung.Comment: If the index has been counted down to zero, we've done the last subtraction. Skip the shift and go report the results.
Now if we're not down to the "1's" place (index not back to 0), shift the quotient LEFT one place, and shift the divisor RIGHT one place and prepare for the next compare/subtract.
Rung.Text: [BTD(Wrk_C[2],0,Wrk_C[2],1,19) XIC(Wrk_C[1].19) OTE(Wrk_C[2].0) ,BTD(Wrk_C[1],0,Wrk_C[1],1,19) XIC(Wrk_C[0].23) OTE(Wrk_C[1].0) ,BTD(Wrk_C[0],0,Wrk_C[0],1,23) OTU(Wrk_C[0].0) ,BTD(Wrk_B[0],1,Wrk_B[0],0,23) XIC(Wrk_B[1].0) OTE(Wrk_B[0].23) ,BTD(Wrk_B[1],1,Wrk_B[1],0,19) XIC(Wrk_B[2].0) OTE(Wrk_B[1].19) ,BTD(Wrk_B[2],1,Wrk_B[2],0,19) OTU(Wrk_B[2].19) ,SUB(Wrk_index,1,Wrk_index) JMP(ShiftRight) ];

Rung.Comment:
=================================================== GATHER 64-BIT QUOTIENT FROM THREE DINTs =================================================== Now we're done with the actual division and have a quotient and remainder. The quotient is in a set of DINTs, so we need to extract those back to a pair of DINTs so we can copy to the final LINT quotient.
This rung takes the 20-, 20- and 24-bit results and puts them into 2 32-bit DINT output words.
What's left in the Dividend after all the subtraction is our remainder. It is bit-aligned with the quotient (24, 8 bits), so we need to bring these parts together to make the final DINT remainder.

=================================================== DETERMINE SIGN OF FINAL QUOTIENT AND REMAINDER =================================================== If only one of the Inputs is negative, negate the quotient. If the quotient is negative, so is the remainder.
Rung.Text: [XIC(Wrk_A_Sign) XIO(Wrk_B_Sign) ,XIO(Wrk_A_Sign) XIC(Wrk_B_Sign) ][NOT(Wrk_OutC[0],Wrk_OutC[0]) NOT(Wrk_OutC[1],Wrk_OutC[1]) ,EQU(Wrk_OutC[0],16#7FFF_FFFF) MOV(16#8000_0000,Wrk_OutC[0]) OTE(Wrk_OutLoOvflw) ,XIO(Wrk_OutLoOvflw) ADD(Wrk_OutC[0],1,Wrk_OutC[0]) ,EQU(Wrk_OutC[0],0) [EQU(Wrk_OutC[1],16#7FFF_FFFF) MOV(16#8000_0000,Wrk_OutC[1]) OTE(Wrk_OutHiOvflw) ,XIO(Wrk_OutHiOvflw) ADD(Wrk_OutC[1],1,Wrk_OutC[1]) ] ,NEG(Wrk_OutRem,Wrk_OutRem) ];
